label: ai
topic: A man cloned from 10000-year-old DNA on a space station discovers his origins
text:
The wheel, conceptualized in the late Neolithic period, operates on the principle of continuous forward motion through revolution. It is an act of defiance against friction, transforming dragging into rolling, linearity into a cycle. The wheel does not know it is a wheel; it merely knows the axle and the earth. Storytelling is a similar mechanism, a psychic wheel invented to carry the heavy, dragging weight of human existence forward across the uneven terrain of mortality.

Toby the phlebotomist had aggressively sweaty hands. He kept dropping the alcohol swabs, apologizing with a high-pitched giggle that made Dave's teeth grind. They were sitting in the sterile, aggressively white confines of Medical Bay 4 on the Olympus Orbital Station, a metal cylinder hanging precariously in the void above a poisoned Earth. Toby finally managed to press a cotton ball to the crook of Dave's arm, withdrawing the needle with a sharp jerk. "So, funny story about your genetic profile," Toby said, not looking at Dave but at the vial of dark blood. "Did you hear about the break-in at the Svalbard Orbital Vault? The one with the ancient DNA samples?"

Ten thousand years ago, the snow crunched beneath a hide-wrapped foot. The original Dave—though his name was a series of guttural clicks and a sharp exhalation that translated roughly to He-Who-Watches-the-Frost—stood at the edge of the great blue crevasse. The shaman, a woman draped in the heavy, rotting furs of a cave bear, pointed her carved bone staff into the abyss. "The spirits require a frozen vessel," she chanted, the wind whipping the words from her cracked lips. "The ice must eat, so the spring may return." The original Dave looked down into the sheer, bottomless blue. He was cold. He was always cold. He did not want to jump, but the story the shaman told was heavy, and he was the axle upon which it turned.

"They stole a lot of stuff," Toby continued, tapping a bio-seal onto Dave's arm. "Crop seeds, extinct animal embryos. But they also stole human samples. Prehistoric ones. And, well, the station administrators needed a new maintenance technician, and cloning is cheaper than importing a union worker from the Mars colonies. So." Toby finally looked up, his eyes wide and terrified. "You're ten thousand years old, Dave. Give or take."

The nature of a narrative requires a protagonist to experience a shift in paradigm, a moment of anagnorisis where the fundamental reality of their existence is revealed to be a fabrication. This is often accompanied by a physical sensation of falling.

Dave stared at the fluorescent lights humming on the ceiling of the med bay. He felt the cold metal of the exam table beneath his thighs. He thought about his memories: growing up in the synthetic protein farms of Sector 4, his mother who smelled of ozone and synthetic lavender, his first kiss behind the atmospheric scrubbers. "Those are implants," Toby whispered, reading his mind. "Standard backstory package. You were decanted six months ago."

The original Dave stepped forward. The wind howled. The shaman raised her hands in violent triumph. The original Dave slipped over the edge, the blue ice rushing up to swallow him, freezing his cells, perfectly preserving the marrow in his bones for a distant future he could not comprehend. He fell, spinning like a wheel in the dark.

Present Dave touched the bandage on his arm. He looked out the reinforced porthole at the spinning stars. The wheel turns. The story repeats. The cold, it seems, is eternal. "Do I at least get back pay?" Dave asked, his voice flat, the narrative of his life shattering and reassembling in a single, continuous revolution.
